Like trying to find a clear path through a muddy park on a wet day. With all this fake stuff floating around, it can make you feel worried, like you can’t trust a single thing you see. Because I don’t spend hours on these things, I try to keep my approach simple, which I think is the best way when you don't have a whole university degree to rely on for facts. It comes down to slowing down and using that common sense we all have. If something looks too shocking or too perfect to be true, it probably is. I think I treat it like looking at a photograph; I stop and really look at the edges, the shadows, and see if it looks right. Is the lighting strange? Does the person look too posed? When I see something that makes my gut clench, I don’t just share it along with my friends, because that’s how the lies spread the fastest. Instead, I take a deep breath, just like before a big football match, and I pause. I ask myself, ‘Who is saying this, and why are they saying it?’ It’s not about getting into a big argument, which would be too much trouble, but just doing a little check. If it’s about politics or something really big, I might just skip it altogether; I don’t need that kind of stress in my life. For the things I do look at, I try to remember that real life, the kind you can touch, like the feel of good rock on a climb or the satisfying click of pieces in a board game, is much more solid than what’s on a screen. We have to be gentle with each other online, and that means not just sharing things, but being careful about what we let into our own minds. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend a few times monthly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: When you’re just scrolling through these social media pages, it feels like there’s so much noise, doesn’t there? Like trying to find a clear path through a muddy park on a wet day. With all this fake stuff floating around, it can make you feel worried, like you can’t trust a single thing you see. Because I don’t spend hours on these things, I try to keep my approach simple, which I think is the best way when you don't have a whole university degree to rely on for facts. It comes down to slowing down and using that common sense we all have. If something looks too shocking or too perfect to be true, it probably is. I think I treat it like looking at a photograph; I stop and really look at the edges, the shadows, and see if it looks right. Is the lighting strange? Does the person look too posed? When I see something that makes my gut clench, I don’t just share it along with my friends, because that’s how the lies spread the fastest. Instead, I take a deep breath, just like before a big football match, and I pause. I ask myself, ‘Who is saying this, and why are they saying it?’ It’s not about getting into a big argument, which would be too much trouble, but just doing a little check. If it’s about politics or something really big, I might just skip it altogether; I don’t need that kind of stress in my life. For the things I do look at, I try to remember that real life, the kind you can touch, like the feel of good rock on a climb or the satisfying click of pieces in a board game, is much more solid than what’s on a screen. We have to be gentle with each other online, and that means not just sharing things, but being careful about what we let into our own minds. It’s about keeping your own spirit calm and grounded, no matter what silly pictures or made-up stories are being thrown around.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 56-year-old, married woman with low formal education, high agreeableness, and a grounded, practical nature (reflected in her hobbies and low neuroticism). She spends little time on social media, suggesting a naturally cautious approach. Her opinion must reflect this practical, non-academic, common-sense skepticism, framed with a gentle, caring tone suitable for someone with high agreeableness. The advice must be actionable but simple, avoiding jargon, and relating to real-world stability (like photography or board games) to align with her grounded traits.
